Tafseer:
Two aspects of a single character: 'Hamz' means to make gestures/mock, and 'lamz' means to find fault/slander. 'Humazah' and 'lumazah' are intensive forms, and further on in this Surah, 'hutamah' also appears on the same pattern. 'Humazah' means one who mocks through gestures, and 'lumazah' means one who continuously finds fault and slanders. Mocking relates mostly to gestures and expressions, while slandering relates to the tongue. These are two aspects of a single character. When someone wants to mock, humiliate, and devalue another in the eyes of others, they use both gestures and the tongue. The humiliation and ridicule created through gestures are occasionally more effective than verbal taunts. Perhaps for this reason, 'humazah' is mentioned first.
The habit of 'hamz and lamz' (mocking and slandering) has always been regarded as a vice in civilized and cultured societies. All heavenly religions prohibit it, and the Holy Qur'an prohibits it in highly clear words:
(And do not defame one another and do not call each other by [offensive] nicknames.) (Al-Hujurat, 49:11).
Opposing the call to truth with cheap weapons: But like modern jahiliyyah, pre-Islamic jahiliyyah also highly promoted this art. In this era, just as newspapers contain humorous columns and cartoons that mock rivals through the language of gestures, similarly in ancient times, there were imitators, clowns, and jesters who were hired to ruin the reputation of noble people and please their patrons. The comprehensive description of the leaders of the Quraysh and their associates in Surah Al-Qalam reveals that they also tried to defeat the Muslims' call to truth and patience through this very weapon which the professional leaders of this era adopt to defeat their rivals; hence, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed to remain alert to their tactics:
(And do not obey every worthless habitual swearer, [or] slanderer, proceeding with abuse, preventer of good, transgressing and sinful, harsh, and moreover, an adopter [of others' lineage]. [Do not obey him] because he is a possessor of wealth and children.) (Al-Qalam, 68:10-14).

A highly comprehensive portrait of miserly capitalists: That is, they tried to silence the advisers and warners through the weapons of mocking and slandering, while remaining occupied in accumulating wealth and continuously counting it. This is a highly comprehensive portrait of those who are greedy and miserly with wealth. Their hearts and minds are constantly occupied with the accounting of their capital: how much profit was gained in a certain business, how much return is expected from a certain capital, how to compensate for a certain loss, and how much the total capital will increase by next year. Such questions constantly dominate their hearts and minds. If anyone mentions that someone spent generously to help the poor and orphans, they mock him, saying: 'He is a boastful show-off, displaying his wealth to impress others. After all, we also spend mounds of wealth, but no one ever hears about it.'

The reflection of their obsession on their inner self: This reflects their obsession on their inner self—showing that they are living under the assumption that this wealth will make them immortal. If they knew that this wealth is temporary and their life is transient, they would hasten to use the wealth for the purpose that would benefit them in their eternal life. But their complete immersion in its love shows that they have found their life in this wealth alone. This mindset is depicted in Surah Ash-Shu'ara:
(Do you construct on every elevation a sign, amusing yourselves, and take for yourselves palaces that you might abide eternally?) (Ash-Shu'ara, 26:128-129).
What reveals a person's inner self is not his tongue, but his conduct of life. The life of a person who regards this world as his ultimate destination is completely different from the life of one who regards this world as a mere path. It is impossible for a person who believes in and seeks the Hereafter to accumulate and count his wealth in the banks and safes of this world; rather, he deposits his wealth with his Lord. Jesus (peace be upon him) stated:
'Store up for yourselves treasures in heaven... for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.'

The fate of the misers' capital: 'Kalla' (No!) is used here to refute the false assumption described in: (He thinks that his wealth will make him immortal). It is stated: never, he and his entire accumulation will be cast into the Crusher. 'Hutamah' is derived from 'hatm', which means to break and crush. This is on the same pattern as 'humazah' and 'lumazah'; hence, it also contains an intensive meaning.

This question is to express its terror—showing that no one should regard it as a minor thing. It is the kindled Fire of God!
Describing this Fire by the attribute 'hutamah' (the Crusher)—meaning that which breaks and crushes—is highly wise because the miserly capitalists of that era accumulated their wealth in the form of gold and silver bars, ornaments, vessels, and gems. To describe the destruction of such wealth, 'the Crusher' is highly appropriate—meaning all of this wealth will be burnt and crushed so that those who regarded it as the guarantee of immortality witness its end.

A specific attribute of the Fire of God: This explains 'hutamah'—showing that it is the kindled Fire of God that mounts directed at the hearts. That is: its specific characteristic will be that it will first seize the hearts in which the love of wealth was so deeply rooted that it left no room for the remembrance of God and the Hereafter. Since its desired food lies within these hearts, its primary attack will be upon them. In this era, specific instruments have been invented to pursue specific targets, finding their prey from a distance and destroying them. The same will be the case with this kindled Fire of Allah: it will mount directed at those hearts that were infatuated with the love of wealth and ignored the rights of the needy.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

This Fire will rage over them and will be closed over them from above so that not a single trace of its heat is lost, but all of it is spent in burning them. 'Awsada-l-bab' means: he closed the door. This Fire will be covered from above like an oven to perform its work with full intensity.

This portrays their disgrace and helplessness: they will be bound to lofty columns with heavy chains in this Fire, unable to move. While columns are mentioned here, Surah Al-Haqqah mentions chains for such criminals:
(Seize him and shackle him, then burn him in Hellfire, then bind him in a chain whose length is seventy cubits. Indeed, he did not believe in Allah, the Most Great, nor did he feel the urge to feed the needy.) (Al-Haqqah, 69:30-34).
